Zwiebelrostbraten with Sauerkraut

Ingredients:

1.  2 pounds boneless pork roast or shoulder
2.  1 1/2 tablespoons of brown sugar
3.  Salt and freshly ground black pepper
4.  1 egg yolk
5.  1 1/2 tablespoons of bread crumbs
6.  1/2 cup of white wine
7.  1 small onion, sliced in half
8.  2 cups of white cabbage, cut in 1 inch squares
9.  6 tablespoons of butter

Prep Time: 45 minutes
Cook Time: 1 1/2 hours

Directions:

1.  Sprinkle the meat with salt and pepper.  Beat the egg yolk with the bread crumbs and brown sugar in a bowl, then spread this mixture on the pork.  Roll the meat in a sheet of plastic wrap, twist it to make a log and tie it in several places with kitchen twine.  Bake the wrapped pork for 1 hour in a preheated 350 degree oven.  When done, remove the plastic wrap and set aside to cool for a few minutes.
2.  While the meat is in the oven, put the wine, onions, and cabbage into a large saucepan.  Bring to a boil, then lower to a simmer and simmer, covered, for 1 hour.  Strain the liquid, reserving the cabbage and onions.  Remove the string from the meat and cut it into slices.  Arrange the pork slices on a platter, place the cabbage and onions on the platter, and pour the broth over the meat.  Dot the meat with butter.

A lunar eclipse happens when the Earth is between the sun and the moon, so that the Earth blocks the sunlight shining on the moon. Rye Bread
Ingredients

4.  When you’re ready to bake, sprinkle 2 to 3 cups of unbleached flour over a clean, unfloured surface and your rye dough.  Roll out the dough into a rectangle, and then roll it up from one side to the other like a burrito.  Repeat this, rolling and folding twice more, and then let the dough rest for 20 minutes.  This makes the dough more elastic and easier to shape.
5.  Cut the dough into three equal pieces.  Roll each piece into a cylinder and then gently roll the cylinder out into a 5 to 6-inch-wide log.  Sprinkle the surface with flour if it sticks to your hands.  To get the proper shape, put a hand on either end of the log and rock it back and forth while rotating your wrists in a motion like turning a crank.
6.  Shape the log into a circle, with the ends overlapping by an inch or two.  Then, starting from one end, gently roll the log forward until it’s shaped like a snail shell.  You’ll have a “bump” in the middle, and some “snail shell” hanging off each end.  Don’t seal the two sides together.  Cover the rolls with a towel and let them rise for another 30 minutes to 2 hours.
7.  Preheat the oven to 375°F.  Once the rolls have finished rising, place them in the oven, seam-side down.  Bake until they’re well-browned, about 35 to 40 minutes.  Let them cool for a minute or two before breaking them apart.
8.  Enjoy your bread!  Serve warm with butter or cream cheese, or slathered with jam.

Assistant: That’s a good question.  The Romans called their basic training, “tirocinium”, and many of the men who went through it did, in fact, go into the military, because “proles”, the bottom class of Roman society, who were not allowed to be citizens or own property, did not have the ability to train to become soldiers and were not allowed to serve.  So the men who were trained in “tirocinium” became the bulk of the military.  This basic training usually lasted for around two years, and they would usually first serve in the “auxilia”, the auxiliary units of the Roman army.  Some of the men who were trained would have been able to then move on to other roles within the Roman military.  That said, not all of them would have.  A man’s rank was generally determined by his social class, so having done basic training did not necessarily guarantee you would get a position in the “auxilia”, but it did make you eligible.

- 6tbsp milk

- 2 tsp fresh parsley, chopped
- 50g plain flour
- salt
- pepper
- a pinch of chili powder
- 1tbsp olive oil
-  
Directions:
- First, take the cubed veal and coat with the fine breadcrumbs, milk, 2 eggs, parsley, nutmeg, and salt and pepper. Make sure to mix well.  Then take a piece of plastic wrap and shape it into a “long rectangular box”.  Mix the veal and crumb mix in it, and roll it up lengthwise (so the length is now greater than the width).  Place it seam side down in the refrigerator.  In a shallow bowl, mix the flour with the chili powder and salt and pepper.  Set it aside.  In a non-stick pan, heat the olive oil to a moderate temperature.  Once it's hot, carefully remove the roll from the plastic wrap and quickly toss it in the flour mixture.  Use a frying basket if you have one, otherwise just carefully turn it over so the seam side is down.  Once browned on both sides, serve with a simple oil and lemon vinaigrette, and of course, eat it all.

You’ll need:

2 tablespoons olive oil

4 garlic cloves

2 medium tomatoes

1 teaspoon ground cumin

1/2 teaspoon ground coriander

1/2 teaspoon ground chili pepper

1/2 teaspoon salt

2 tablespoons red wine

8 eggs

6 ounces feta cheese, diced

4-6 tablespoons roughly chopped parsley

2 tablespoons roughly chopped mint

Directions

1. Preheat oven to 375 degrees Fahrenheit.

2. Grease a 13-by-9-inch baking dish.

3. Heat olive oil in a large pan over medium-high heat. Add garlic and cook until fragrant and starting to brown, about a minute. Add tomatoes, spices, and salt, and cook for 3 minutes. Remove from heat and scrape into a blender, then pulse until mostly smooth.

4. Place mixture in baking dish and coat the bottom of the dish with it.

5. Crack the eggs over the mixture in the baking dish, and scatter feta and herbs on top.

6. Pour the wine over it.

7. Cover the baking dish with aluminum foil and bake in oven for 15 minutes.

8. Uncover and cook for another 5-10 minutes.

9. Allow to cool for 10 minutes before serving.

I think that foot warmers really aren’t a good idea for a whole bunch of reasons.

The main one is that they work by heating up the air next to your skin.  This means the area near your feet will be hot and dry, which is kind of a bad thing.

The second is that heating the air near your feet only delays the inevitable.  That is, unless you can keep your feet at a constant temperature all winter long, your feet will inevitably end up freezing.

The third is that for some people the extra heat can be too much, which can actually damage the feet.

And the fourth is that foot warmers are not particularly safe -- they can overheat and burn you if the foot warmer is too near your foot.

Ingredients:

1/4 lb ground meat (chicken, lamb, beef)

2 tbsp flour

2 cloves garlic, minced

2 onions, finely chopped

1 large egg, lightly beaten

1 tbsp fresh parsley, chopped

1 tbsp fresh mint, chopped

salt, black pepper

200 grams bread, crusts removed, cubed

1 large can white beans, drained, rinsed and dried

1 tomato, finely chopped

1/2 cup plain yogurt

Method:

1.    Put flour into a large bowl and season it with salt and pepper.

2.    Put meat, garlic, onions, egg and parsley into a food processor and process until meat is finely minced and mixed with the onions. 

3.    Put meat mixture into a large bowl, along with bread cubes, beans, tomato and yogurt, mix well.

4.    Sprinkle mixture with salt and pepper to taste.

5.    Take a large piece of parchment paper and spread out the meat mixture. Lay another large piece of parchment paper on top.

6.    Roll out the meat mixture and place into an 8-10” diameter lightly oiled casserole dish.

7.    Cook under the broiler until the top turns golden brown, about 8-10 minutes.

8.    Cut into squares and serve.

Assistant: in principle you should take antibiotics if you have a serious bacterial infection, which means an infection that can be cured by antibiotics but could instead kill you. In practice, though, doctors will often prescribe antibiotics even when they don’t have a very good idea of whether you have an infection, or even if it’s really worth your time and money to take the antibiotics and avoid the possible side effects.  If you’re in a hospital, a surgeon or infectious disease doctor will decide whether or not to give you antibiotics, and if they do, they’ll tell you exactly why.  There are also guidelines, or “evidence-based” approaches, which say that certain conditions have a low enough risk of infection that they don’t need antibiotics, and other conditions have a high enough risk that it’s usually better to take them even if there’s not really a bacterial infection there.  It’s a good idea to read more about those guidelines, or talk with your doctor about them.

1 1⁄2 cups warm water

2 teaspoons active dry yeast

1 1⁄2 cups lukewarm water

3 cups whole wheat flour

3 cups white flour

1 1⁄2 tablespoons salt

Directions:

1. Pour warm water into a big bowl and stir in yeast until dissolved.

2. Stir in lukewarm water, whole wheat flour and white flour and salt.

3. Mix together, cover with a kitchen towel and allow to ferment overnight.

4. Knead with enough flour so that dough is not sticky. Shape dough into two balls and let rise under a kitchen towel for about an hour.

5. Preheat oven to 400°F. Flatten the dough to 2 1⁄2" thick rounds.

6. Place the dough on a baking sheet and slash the surface in several places with a knife. Bake until dark golden brown and sound hollow when tapped on the bottom, about 30 minutes.  Cool on a wire rack.  Wrap in plastic and freeze to eat later.

The reason for the introduction of these coins or candy was that the fairies were believed to visit the homes of children while they were sleeping, and that if a child was awake, the fairy would not come.  In medieval Europe, in fact, the tradition of leaving coins for fairies was widespread, and in places like Britain, many coins were left in the home of children so that they would be in place when the fairies were visiting.  In this way, children were educated about the fairy world, because they were required to learn from their parents about the right way to show respect for the fairies.  In many cultures, it was common for a small part of the tooth to be left in a cup or bowl as an offering.  Some would leave it on a plate, or even hide it under a pillow.

You can also find additional recipes at the website of their company, Moroccan-Flavors.com.

“Cut off the skin from the shoulder of a lamb and slice very thinly, not less than three layers. Take two hot chilies, also as thinly sliced. Wash the meat and leave it for an hour in salt water and drain. Then put it in a pot and add a bit of water; if the meat doesn’t cover all the water add enough until it’s covered. Add water until the meat is completely covered and put on a heat for a couple of minutes. Then turn down the heat and cook it very slowly, and when the meat is very well cooked take it out and let it cool off. Then put it in a large pan with the chili and when it’s cold, add as much as one tablespoon of olive oil, then salt it, and cook the meat for ten minutes in a pot that’s simmering on the stove. Take it out and put it in a grinder, and turn it on; the last one or two minutes take it off and stir the ingredients in a bowl. Then take a pan with a little water, bring it to a boil and add about one or two tablespoons of flour, then let it cook for a minute and then add a few tablespoons of olive oil. You will get a delicious spread for meat that’s called Za’atar, and you will also get a beautiful stew like a bouillabaisse. When the bouillabaisse is done and the chili and meat is well mixed, stir the water and flour together in the bowl. Then add the stock from the bouillabaisse and cook for about a minute. Stir in the olive oil and serve it. The taste is magnificent and many people can’t believe you can make this meal from scratch.”

Human: How is particle board made?

Assistant: It’s manufactured in several different ways.  The basic idea is the cardboard substrate is sprayed with a resin or another solid material that is typically wood fiber or particles of plastic.  One variation is called “wet lay”, in which the resins are sprayed directly onto the cardboard.  This approach can be used to make the substrate more water resistant, although wet lay produces surfaces that are more prone to delamination, especially over time.

Another approach is called “dry lay”, in which the substrate is impregnated with wood powder or other powder.  This is more economical, because it requires less resin.  The substrate is then covered with a layer of a wet-resin, which when it dries, adheres to the substrate, but not to the wood-particle material on top of it.  This means the particle-board can be finished with a clear coat of resin to seal the board and prevent water absorption.

When metal screw has been machined too fine and does not have enough “grease land”, metal particles from screw itself penetrate into grooves of the workpiece and remain inside.  The screw is then pulled out of the workpiece but the particles left behind can create an almost impossible screw to remove from the workpiece.  

These particles can be forced out of the wood by rotating the workpiece in the other direction using a twisting motion to loosen the screw, then using a screwdriver to tap the bottom of the screw until all the particles fall out.  A hammer can be used to break off the stub of the screw, making it possible to remove the remaining screw.

Shepherd’s Pie

Ingredients

9 oz. extra-lean ground beef

1 medium onion, diced

2 large celery stalks, diced

2 cloves garlic, minced

1 medium carrot, diced

1/2 cup frozen mixed vegetables (peas and corn)

1 cup frozen peas

2 cups ready-to-serve mashed potatoes

2-1/4 cups milk

1 tablespoon Dijon mustard

1/2 teaspoon ground black pepper

1/2 teaspoon kosher salt

1/2 cup grated cheddar cheese

Heat oven to 350°F. Spray an 8-inch square baking dish with cooking spray. In a large skillet, brown ground beef over medium-high heat. Drain fat. Add onion, celery, garlic, and carrot. - 1⁄2 cup white sugar
- 1⁄4 cup peanut oil
- 2 TBS lime juice
- 1 TBS finely grated ginger root
- 1 tsp black pepper
- 1⁄2 tsp chili powder

Method:


Wash peanuts and dry well.

Then put them in a blender and blend until smooth.

Add sugar, oil, lime juice, ginger, black pepper and chili powder.

Blend well.

Groundnut Sauce is ready.

Here’s what the recipe will involve:
-Mix all the dry ingredients in a large bowl. You’ll need salt, yeast, sugar, and butter.
-Add 2 cups of hot water and stir to combine.
-Stir in 2 cups of bread flour, making sure everything is well-combined.

-Remove the dough from the fridge and give it another punch to knock some of the air out, then transfer it to a lightly-floured surface and knead it until the dough is smooth and not sticky. Use a bowl scraper to transfer the dough to a lightly-floured surface.
-Preheat the oven to 425 degrees.
-Using a rolling pin, roll the dough into a rectangle about 12 inches long and 8 inches wide, and 1⁄4 of an inch thick.
-Spread 1 cup of tomato sauce over the surface of the dough.
-Sprinkle 1 cup of shredded mozzarella cheese over the surface of the dough.
-Using a pizza cutter, cut the dough into 3 rows, each 12 inches long.
-Sprinkle 1⁄4 cup of grated parmesan cheese over the surface of the dough.
-Starting from the end closest to you, fold the first pizza into a triangle, pulling the dough up and towards the center, and then fold the first two triangles in half toward the center. Repeat with the remaining rows.
-Using a spatula, place the dough into a lightly-oiled pan, then top with additional mozzarella cheese.
-Using the spatula, top the pan with another pan, and place both pans in the oven.
-Bake until the crust has browned and the cheese is melted, about 20-25 minutes.

Gudeg Jogja Adonan

1 kg jackfruit

2 tsp sugar

1 tbsp lime juice
1/2 tsp salt

Human: What is a logical fallacy?

Assistant: That’s a tricky question. A common definition of a logical fallacy is an argument that can be proved logically invalid. But in practice, that just seems to be a catch-all for any kind of faulty reasoning. I personally find it more helpful to classify fallacies by the kinds of reasoning that they make invalid. 
For example, there’s “equivocation” (also known as “amphiboly”), where words have a meaning that shifts over time. So I might say “If I went to the store, I’d buy a cake,” where I’m equivocating on the meaning of “I”. In this example, the original “I” refers to me, but later the “I” refers to you. The problem is that my statement doesn’t apply to you at all. This kind of fallacy is very common in everyday speech, so maybe it’s not a good example, but it’s one that comes to mind.
rejected:

Assistant: Do you mean in everyday language, or what?  The word “fallacy” comes from the Greek word “to fall”.  A “fallacy” is a mistake or misstep in reasoning.  And a “logical fallacy” is a mistake or misstep in reasoning that might arise from logical flaws.  You can think of logic as a method for reasoning that’s informed by strict rules for reasoning.  There are lots of different ways a fallacy can come about, but usually the rules of logic are not followed exactly, which means some inferences could have been false.  For example, one very common fallacy is that of “equivocation”: to equivocate is to take different meanings of a word at different times, which then causes you to draw an incorrect conclusion.

(3 whole ducks)

Preparation:
For the first day:  Slice one duck into six parts (slicing from tail to breast along the backbone), leave the other three ducks whole.  Wash the pieces in salt water.

For the second day:  Smear the duck pieces with ginger juice (very finely sliced ginger), salt, sugar, soy sauce and sesame oil, and let them sit for a few hours.  When you’re ready to cook, put them in a deep pot and fill it with water to cover, add a pinch of black pepper, star anise, and a little bit of Sichuan peppercorn and cook them at a slow boil for about 45 minutes.

For the third day:  Put some soy sauce, five spice powder, black bean paste, honey and sesame oil in the pot and boil them in the duck for 5 minutes.  For the other three ducks, wash them in salt water and cover with a marinade of ginger juice, soy sauce, vinegar, sugar, and sesame oil, and let them sit for about 15 minutes.

Final preparation:  Cook the three remaining ducks for 20 minutes at low heat, with the marinade in the pot.  When you’re ready to serve, take the cooked pieces out of the pot and reheat them on the fire.  Put the pot of boiled duck on a table and pour the liquid from it into a sauce bowl.

Tips:  After you take the cooked duck pieces out of the pot, place them on a chopping block and chop them into small, bite-sized pieces.  Serve with ginger-vinegar sauce, rice wine and tea.

Game description and game rules:

Table tennis is a sport played between two people (singles or doubles) on a table divided by a net.

Each player uses a paddle and a ball. The game is played in two courts and is usually played on a hard, wood, plastic, concrete or similar type table and a height-adjustable net. A line in the center of the table demarcates the area where the ball must be hit.

Table tennis is one of the most popular racket sports in the world. It is governed by the International Table Tennis Federation (ITTF) and its official rules are codified in the International Table Tennis Federation's Rules of Table Tennis (ITTF).

The goal of the game is to hit a ball (a lightweight rubber or plastic sphere) back and forth across the table (or "net") with a long paddle (or "racquet"). The players must not allow the ball to bounce more than twice on their side of the table. They may not touch the ball with any part of the body (other than the racket) above the waist. The ball is served underhand and must be returned by a "slap" or "drive" (by striking the ball "flat" with the blade of the racket, rather than striking the ball "lob" or "topspin" with the edge of the racket).

Because table tennis is played on a table rather than on a floor (like floorball, or racquetball) or a court (like most other racket sports), the length of the rally can continue much longer than in other racket sports. The two players can employ many different table tennis shots: serve, smash, smash return, drop shot, chop, lob, float, pick-up, push, block, flick, tap, deflection, flick return, and finger spin.

A match is won by the first player to score 21 points, except when playing a "gold medal match" (a match for the championship of a tournament) or "salon match" (a casual match), when the game continues until one player or team scores 2 points more than their opponent, the two players having a "second game" (similar to a tennis tiebreak), and if it results in a draw (tie) the players have a "third game" (similar to a tiebreak) which results in the winner of the match. a) Create a profile on the Bone Marrow Donor Registry. 

b) Sign up for medical tests. You need to have your arm cleansed with an alcohol wipe and your blood drawn.  Once you pass these two tests, you’re matched to donors for a “transplant” if you don’t have a match in the national registry already. 

c) At this point you are a “eligible” donor. This means you’re up for consideration to donate your bone marrow if a “potential” donor has been found but no match. 
There is also a physical exam and more testing to be done by a doctor to make sure you’re healthy enough to donate your bone marrow.  Donors can be male or female, ages 18 and over.

Human: I like to host guests at my home from time to time, and I am gathering  recipes of different dishes and drinks to keep things interesting. I am interested in trying some Sicilian dishes. Can you give me a recipe for Stuffed Artichokes?

Assistant: This sounds really interesting! Stuffed artichokes are delicious, and I’m glad you asked for that one!

Artichokes have a lot of tough skin, so I recommend par-boiling them first.  Place the artichokes in a pot of boiling water for 10-15 minutes.  Then when the artichokes are cool enough to handle, use a sharp knife to cut off the tops and root ends.   You can reserve them for stuffing later, or throw them away, it’s up to you.  Then, use your fingers to spread apart the inner leaves.  You should be able to feel the fuzzy inner leaves, the ones where the stem was attached.  Once you’ve got your hands into the center, you should see the fuzzy center.  Scoop that out, and you can use it for stuffing.  Now that the artichoke is opened, you’re ready to start stuffing it.  

In general, stuffed artichokes can be stuffed with any filling you like, but the classic Sicilian stuffing is this:

- 1/3 cup chopped onions
- 4 ounces fresh mushrooms, diced
- 1 clove garlic, minced
- 2 tablespoons chopped parsley
- 1 teaspoon salt
- 1/2 teaspoon black pepper
- 1/2 cup chopped tomatoes
- 2 cups spinach leaves, well washed and patted dry

To stuff the artichoke, line the artichoke up to hold it upright, and hold it with the stem side down on the countertop.  Remove any smaller outer leaves and hold the artichoke in place, stem-side down, while you work.  In this position, the outer leaves will act as a sort of plate to hold the stuffing.  Add a few spoonfuls of filling into the center, then fold the outer leaves up like a “roof”.  Add the rest of the filling and then close the artichoke off completely.  Once the artichoke is stuffed, place it in a pot of water and cook for 30 minutes or so until tender.  If you cook it for less time, the artichoke is likely to be too crunchy to be pleasant to eat.

Assistant: Sure! Basbousa is a traditional Egyptian sweet or savory cake.  It is usually made using wheat flour and yeast, and traditionally has a filling with either nuts or dates.  There are many regional variations, so here is a simple recipe that I’ve found on the Internet: 

-   9 oz. butter
-   5 1⁄2 oz. all-purpose flour
-   1⁄4 tsp. baking soda
-   1⁄2 tsp. baking powder
-   2 tbsp. sugar
-   1 tsp. orange blossom water (or any flavorful perfume, such as jasmine or rose water)
-   1⁄2 tsp. rosewater
-   Pinch of salt
-   1 egg
-   1 egg yolk
-   About 6 to 8 tbsp. of water or milk

Preheat your oven to 350 Fahrenheit.

Sift the flour, baking soda and baking powder in a medium bowl.  In a separate bowl, beat the butter and sugar using a mixer until pale and fluffy.

Add in the flour mixture and orange blossom water (or any other flavouring) to the butter and sugar, then beat on medium speed until combined.  Finally, add in the eggs and mix until smooth.

Prepare a 9 x 9 inch baking tin by greasing it and lining it with parchment paper.  The paper must go up to the sides, but not cover the bottom.

Place a small amount of water (about 2 to 3 tbsp.) in the bottom of the tin, and then pour in the batter.

Bake the basbousa for 15 to 20 minutes, or until cooked through.  When it comes out of the oven, cool for a few minutes.  Then place the baking tin on a flat surface, and the parchment paper will stay flat on top of the cake.  Using a warm, wet knife, carefully cut the cake into squares.  If it’s too hot, the cake will fall apart.

3 cups water
1/2 tsp salt

Human: A friend was talking to me about "ions" but I am unsure what they are. Can you help me to understand this concept/what an ion actually is?

Assistant: The word "ion" comes from the Greek word for "move" and it refers to any atom that has gained or lost electrons. For example, if you dissolve a tiny amount of table salt in water, the sodium and chlorine atoms in the salt will react with some of the water's hydrogen atoms and they will start to share the electrons between them. In a nutshell, they're exchanging electrons. When this happens, the sodium atom will gain a positive charge, while the chlorine atom loses one. They now have a "positive charge" or "a +1 charge" or "a positive ion" and they have a "negative charge" or "a -1 charge" or "a negative ion". When atoms with more electrons in their outer shells gain electrons they become ions with a +1 charge, and when atoms with less electrons gain electrons they become ions with a -1 charge. So the salt water will be an electrolyte solution with dissolved sodium and chlorine ions.

But in general, we often talk about ions as collections of atoms or molecules that have gained or lost one or more electrons. They are very common, and there are many practical examples of them. For example, molecules in our food that form acids, like citric acid in orange juice, are ions. The compound that keeps your food fresh, called "Sodium Bicarbonate" (the stuff you buy in little sacks at the grocery store) is also an ion, but it's a "salt", and the "sodium" and "bicarbonate" atoms are sharing electrons.  

When atoms gain or lose electrons, it’s because there's an interaction between the electrons and the atom, for example if they are too close together, or if the atom is "an attractive enough environment" for the electrons. This is all quite abstract, but it’s a cool thing to think about.

In the early 2nd century, Christians chose to accept the Gospel of John as canonical for two reasons: they read the book as their most authoritative account of the person and teachings of Jesus and as a means of supporting his resurrection. The fourth and fifth chapters contain a distinct liturgical dialog, in which Jesus has a discussion with the Samaritan woman at the well. Here he speaks to her about her deep spirituality, about her past sins, and about the future coming of the Messiah, and is identified as the Messiah. In chapters six through ten, Jesus feeds 5,000 people with five loaves of bread and two fish. He also walks on water. He then performs a miracle of healing a man born blind. Here the narrative turns to Jesus’ ministry in Jerusalem and his entry into the city itself.

Assistant: No.  The USA was founded by a Puritan group of Protestants, but it didn’t adopt the religion of the Puritans until much later, and it was always a secular state.  The Puritans observed the Sabbath on Sunday, and the Puritans only had a small influence in the early history of the USA.  It’s difficult to trace the origins of closing stores on Sunday, but one early and short-lived attempt at forcing the Sabbath on people in the 1800s was motivated by the Protestant ideal that people should spend Sunday focusing on spiritual activities.  By the mid-1800s, when the Sunday closing law was made, there was not a lot of pressure from that standpoint, but the church had begun to advocate for Sunday closing laws as a way of counteracting the negative effects of industrialization on the day of rest.  Even after that shift, closing stores on Sunday was not always possible, since the religious Sunday was not always chosen for observance.  And as industrialization accelerated and mechanization made it possible to operate stores on Sunday, the law was not enforced as much as people liked.  The day of rest was also being violated by stores that stayed open all day on Sunday, so closing stores on Sundays became an effort to protect the Sabbath for all citizens.

Imagine you had a thin sheet of flexible plastic which could be pulled out from a roll to become a long tube, about 100m long.  We call this a “wormhole”, as in, it’s a flexible wormhole.  If you were floating in space, and grabbed one end of it and held it, then the other end would stretch to the ground, into the ground and back out again. Tachyons are things that accelerate faster than light, and as they pass by, objects may seem to move or “rotate” as they pass, even though the objects themselves are not moving.

Human: Can you describe how an autonomous car works?

Assistant: One way to think about it is this:  You can make a car without a human driver in two different ways.  The first is to put a robot in the car, which is kind of like a remote-control helicopter.  The robot would have a video camera, some artificial intelligence, and some sensors.  When you are driving, you would be able to see out the window and steer and accelerate and brake with a joystick or other controls, so it’s like you are driving, but you aren’t.  You’d control the robot, and the robot would control the car.  If you wanted to be super safe, you could build an extra sensor in the car that would only drive in the safest conditions, and when there were hazards, or if the car sensed the driver wasn’t doing what they should, then the robot would actually take over control of the car and drive it instead.  This is the safer way, but it would also be kind of slow because the robot would just be doing the exact same thing that you would have done, and if you messed up, the robot would also mess up.

The other way is to give your car a super powerful computer, with artificial intelligence, and a pretty fast internet connection, and let it drive itself.  This car would also have some sensors, but it wouldn’t really need them, because it would always have access to so much more information than you do.  When you are driving, you would choose where you want to go, and your car would use all of its data to come up with the best route and way to get there.  The computer would make all the decisions about how to handle unexpected events and road conditions, and would do the braking and steering and everything.  It’s kind of like having a flying car that is in control of everything, and you just decide where you want to go.  Most people use cars like this, because they’re pretty good and most of the time you don’t really want to do all of that work and risking making a mistake.

Assistant: The safety of autonomous cars is a very controversial topic right now.  As a car moves along a road, it’s constantly receiving information from its sensors: can it see nearby traffic, or other road users?  And it’s constantly receiving information from its GPS system: what is the speed limit in this location, where are the red lights?  And it’s always making decisions: how fast should it go?  Does it have the right-of-way?  The driver is an important part of all of this decision-making, but it’s one of the most challenging areas of technology.  Autonomous cars are often compared to flying an airplane, where you just get on and have your fun, and there’s a crew in charge.  The computers do the hard work of flying.  But you can’t just look at something and tell if it’s safe to drive.  There’s always a tradeoff.  For example, we can take a picture of an intersection and see how many cars have stopped there at different times, and then estimate how many cars are likely to arrive in the future.  But no image will ever be able to tell you exactly how many cars will be in an intersection at any given time, because each car is in motion, and each driver is different and can change his or her mind in the moment.  In the end, the computer is probably making hundreds of judgments, some of them simple, and some of them incredibly complicated.  And humans make judgments too.  That’s why the driver needs to be paying attention, and must still be able to see clearly, and still must have his or her own ideas about what to do.

Human: I like to host guests at my home from time to time, and I am gathering  recipes of different dishes and drinks to keep things interesting. I am interested in trying some Pakistani dishes. Can you give me a recipe for Aloo Ki Tikki?

Assistant: Sure, Aloo ki Tikki (recipe below). I recommend buying all the ingredients ahead of time. Then you will need to start with step two and have everything chopped and ready by the time you get to step four.

1 onion, finely chopped (5 tablespoons)

5 potatoes (red skinned if possible, they have a sweet taste), cubed

2-3 tomatoes, finely chopped (6-8 tbsp)

1 tsp ginger, grated

1 tsp salt

2-3 cloves of garlic, crushed

1⁄2 tsp red chili powder

2-3 tbsp cumin powder

1 tbsp coriander powder

1 tbsp garam masala

2 tbsp lemon juice

1⁄2 cup (2 oz) coriander, chopped (optional)

Cooking oil

----

Heat cooking oil in a deep pan or kadhai, add the onion, and fry it until it turns light golden.

Remove the onions and drain on a paper towel. Leave the oil in the pan.

Add the tomatoes, ginger, garlic, and salt, stir and cook for about 3 minutes.

Add the chili powder, cumin, coriander, and garam masala. Stir well, and cook for 2 minutes.

Add the potatoes, and cook for 2 minutes.

Turn the heat down, and mix well. Add 2-3 tbsp water, and cook for 8-10 minutes. Keep stirring and checking.

Add the lemon juice. Remove from heat. Mix in the coriander and let it cool a little.

Cut potatoes and tomato and shape into balls.

Heat some cooking oil in a deep pan or kadhai. When the oil is hot, add the potatoes. Fry till they turn light golden brown.

In a paper towel, drain the fried potatoes. Leave the oil in the pan.

In the same oil, add the onion, tomatoes, and fried potatoes.

Stir well, and add 2-3 tbsp water. Cook for 3-4 minutes.

----

Ok so my colleague found a recipe on a site called “onegoodthingbyjillee”.com. Here is their recipe for Swordfish a la Siciliana:

Swordfish a la Siciliana

Ingredients:

1 pound swordfish steaks (alternately, use tuna steaks or chicken cutlets)
1/4 cup extra-virgin olive oil
1/4 cup red wine vinegar
1/4 cup fresh-squeezed lemon juice
3 cloves garlic, finely chopped
1/2 cup fresh flat-leaf parsley, finely chopped
1 teaspoon fresh oregano
1 teaspoon crushed red chili flakes
3/4 cup plain bread crumbs (high-quality, fresh bread crumbs will work best; if they’re very dry, add 1 tablespoon water to the crumbs to help them stick to the fish)
salt and freshly ground pepper

Method:

1. Place a skillet over medium heat and add the oil, vinegar, lemon juice, and garlic.
2. Bring the mixture to a boil, and reduce the heat to low.
3. Let simmer while you make the breading.
4. In a shallow bowl, mix together the parsley, oregano, crushed red chili flakes, and bread crumbs.
5. Season with salt and pepper, to taste.
6. Dip the swordfish into the sauce, coating both sides.
7. Press the fish into the bread crumb mixture to cover completely.
8. Place the swordfish on a greased cookie sheet and bake at 375°F for 15 minutes.
9. Once the fish is cooked through, remove it from the oven and cover with the remaining bread crumb mixture.
10. Return it to the oven and bake for an additional 5 minutes.
11. Allow to cool for 5 minutes before serving, with lemon wedges on the side for garnish.

Preheat oven to 375 degrees. Cover the corn husks in warm water, then drain them and let them dry a little. Cut the tops and bottoms off the corn husks so they will lie flat. Fold the sides of the husk up over the masa and, holding the husk, form a rough square or rectangle.

Put the husks in a skillet filled with a thin layer of lard or oil. Fry them over low heat, turning frequently, for 10 minutes. Remove them from the skillet and place them in a warm place (such as an oven with the light turned on) for an hour to dry. Heat the lard in the skillet and fry the nacatamales again. Turn once.

A few minutes before serving, place the fried nacatamales in a 375 degree oven.

Remove the husks from the oven, and using a sharp knife, make an incision in each nacatamale and pour about a tablespoon of lard on top. Human: How can I treat sore thighs from running?

Assistant: Running is a fantastic workout, and most people will get sore legs at first.  After a few runs, you’ll find that your muscles and skin toughen up and you can go further and run faster.  There are some basic things you can do that will help you get better faster:

1.  Ice baths, where you fill a large bath tub with water, add ice, and soak your legs in the bath for ten to fifteen minutes.  This will make the skin and muscles tighter, and will help them heal faster.
2.  Massage.  Most people are not good at self-massage, but with a little practice you can rub your muscles to ease pain, and speed healing.
3.  Wearing compression clothing can help prevent chafing, while also giving a bit of added support for your muscles and joints.
4.  If you do a lot of running, it’s a good idea to invest in a new pair of shoes every few months.  Runners often put a lot of stress on the plantar fascia, which is a small and often ignored tendon that stretches from the bottom of the foot up to the base of the toes.  You can protect it with a new pair of shoes, as well as some custom orthotics for your feet that might be recommended by a physical therapist.  New shoes can also be more comfortable.
5.  Take care of your feet.  Runners usually end up getting heel blisters, or some form of calluses on their feet.  You can relieve this by massaging the bottom of your feet and applying antiseptic cream, or you can get insoles to make it more comfortable.  Good shoes can also help.
6.  If you’re getting hip and knee pain, it’s worth seeing a doctor for some exercises and advice.  Your joints are weak, and stretching and warm-up exercises can be especially important.

Finally, add some water to the pan with the hare, and stir in the prunes to the hare’s cooking juices. Put the hare back into the oven, and cook it for a further ten minutes.

Remove the hare from the oven, and cover it with a large, flat piece of lettuce. Set the hare onto a serving platter and pour some of the thick sauce over it. Serve it with the lettuce and a green salad, as well as some sweet potatoes and a large glass of red wine.

Cook this for half an hour or until it’s very soft. Once it’s soft, it’s ready to prepare.  You need to wash the cassava and then grate it into a pot. Prepare a large, slightly deep pan of boiling water. Cut some washed rice in small pieces with a food processor. Throw them in the boiling water, and put a lid on top to keep the heat in and the rice from drying out. Cook for about 10 minutes until the rice is cooked through. Drain the rice.

Add a little water to the fufu cooking pan, then mix the mango sauce with the cooked rice. It is best to add the mango sauce about half way through. A good thing to do is to make this mixture by hand, so that the rice is somewhat broken up. After the rice is mixed in, the fufu will start to clump together. Continue adding water as needed to keep it from clumping. When done, serve with a sprinkle of chili powder.

Assistant: This is hard to predict, since humans have never been in space for such a long time, but I have gathered some information here:

Brain: The brain gets smaller and memory is affected

Sex hormones: Testosterone levels may be affected, but it’s not clear what effect this would have

Urinary tract: Androgens (male sex hormones) decrease. The space medicine website says that astronauts have trouble retaining urine, but you might be okay.

Vocal cord: The space medicine website also says that there’s an increased risk of vocal cord injuries, but there’s no data on the effects.

Muscles: Increased risk of atrophy in muscles.

Vision: A decreased response to light sensitivity

Genes: Telomere length is decreased.

Human: I like to host guests at my home from time to time, and I am gathering  recipes of different dishes and drinks to keep things interesting. I am interested in trying some Belgium dishes. Can you give me a recipe for Chicken Waterzooi?

Assistant: Chicken Waterzooi
One of Belgium’s favorite dishes is waterzooi. Traditionally served as a main dish at breakfast, it also makes a great first course for dinner. Chicken Waterzooi is a richly flavored, warm dish, which pairs perfectly with a crisp green salad.
Ingredients:
2 cups chicken broth (or chicken stock)
1 onion, sliced
1 large carrot, sliced
4 tablespoons unsalted butter
4 cups skim milk
1/2 teaspoon fresh thyme leaves, chopped
1/2 cup fresh mushrooms, sliced (optional)
3 cups diced cooked chicken (canned will work just fine if you don’t have time to roast a whole chicken)
Salt and pepper to taste
4-8 tablespoons heavy cream (optional)
Preparation:
In a large saucepan, heat the chicken stock and add the onion and carrots. Bring to a boil over medium-high heat. Reduce heat to low, cover and simmer until the vegetables are tender, about 10-15 minutes.
Strain the vegetables, reserving the chicken stock.
In a separate saucepan, melt the butter over low heat. Whisk in the milk, thyme and mushrooms (if using). Cook until the milk is scalded and the mushroom pieces are tender.
Stir in the chicken and reserved stock. Season with salt and pepper, cover and simmer for 5 minutes. Stir in heavy cream to taste, and serve immediately.
